skill.name: virtual-environment
skill.description: Detect project dependency files and guide setup of local isolated environments for Python, Node.js, Ruby, and Go projects.
skill.summary: Set up project dependency isolation with clear checks and environment commands.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many backtick execution patterns, but the evidence is Markdown inline code and fenced shell examples, not Ruby execution. The skill is still medium risk because it tells agents to run package installation commands and includes a troubleshooting cleanup command that should require user confirmation.

medium_findings.title: Package Installation Commands Require User Confirmation
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends commands such as pip install, poetry install, pipenv install, conda install, npm install, yarn install, and pnpm install. These commands are legitimate for environment setup, but they execute package manager behavior from project metadata and can run dependency lifecycle code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly present in shell examples and are intended to be run during setup. The risk is operational and supply-chain related, not evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick findings are Markdown table entries, fenced code blocks, and inline command references. No Ruby code execution syntax or hidden execution logic was found in the reviewed SKILL.md content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown documentation and the cited syntax is visible as code formatting or shell examples. There is no Ruby source file or executable Ruby block in the skill.

low_findings.title: Environment File References Are Documentation, Not Secret Access
low_findings.description: The reported environment file alerts refer to the .env directory name in virtual environment naming and gitignore examples. The skill does not instruct reading secrets from a .env file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show directory matching, naming guidance, and gitignore entries. I did not find evidence of secret collection or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Checks Are Limited to Local Project Paths
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings are local existence checks for node_modules with stderr redirection. They do not read sensitive device files or traverse outside the project.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command only checks whether node_modules exists and suppresses errors. This is a normal project setup check with minimal security impact.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Has No Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The static blocker alert points to the frontmatter description. That line describes dependency isolation and does not contain a weak cryptographic algorithm or crypto operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is plain metadata text about virtual environments. I did not find evidence of cryptographic code in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Dependency Cleanup Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The troubleshooting table suggests removing node_modules before reinstalling dependencies. This is usually recoverable but should not run without user approval because it deletes local files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and destructive within a dependency directory. The scope is limited to node_modules, so the risk is moderate rather than high.

content.value_statement: Projects often fail when dependencies leak across local environments. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code detect project type and choose the right isolation workflow.
actual_capabilities: Identifies common project files such as requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, Pipfile, package.json, Gemfile, and go.mod.
Checks for existing Python virtual environment folders and Node.js node_modules directories.
Provides setup commands for venv, Poetry, Pipenv, Conda, npm, yarn, and pnpm workflows.
Suggests virtual environment naming conventions and gitignore entries.
Includes troubleshooting guidance for missing interpreters, package conflicts, and dependency folder issues.
limitations: Does not validate package integrity or audit dependency security.
Does not create environments automatically without a tool running the documented commands.
Does not cover every language ecosystem or custom build system.
Requires user review before package installation or cleanup commands are executed.

use_cases.title: Start a Python Repository
use_cases.description: Detect the dependency manager and set up a clean local environment before installing packages.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Node.js Project
use_cases.description: Check whether dependencies are installed and choose npm, yarn, or pnpm based on project files.

use_cases.title: Standardize Onboarding Steps
use_cases.description: Create consistent environment setup guidance for contributors joining a mixed-language repository.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to inspect the current project files and tell me which environment setup workflow applies.

prompt_templates.title: Create Python Environment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to recommend the safest Python virtual environment setup for this repository. Ask before running install commands.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Node Dependencies
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to check whether this Node.js project has dependencies installed and propose the correct package manager steps.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Setup Instructions
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review our repository setup process and produce a concise environment checklist for new contributors.

output_examples.input: Check this repository before I install dependencies.
output_examples.output: Detected a Python project with requirements.txt. Recommended workflow: create a .venv folder, activate it, then install requirements after confirmation.

output_examples.input: Help me start work on this Node.js app.
output_examples.output: Detected package.json. Check for an existing node_modules directory, then use the package manager selected by the repository lockfile.

output_examples.input: What should contributors do before running tests?
output: Confirm the project type from dependency files.
Create or activate the expected isolated environment.
Install dependencies only after reviewing the package manager command.
Ensure generated dependency folders are ignored by version control.

best_practices: Review dependency files before running package manager commands.
Use .venv for Python projects to avoid confusion with environment variable files.
Confirm cleanup commands before deleting generated dependency folders.
anti_patterns: Installing dependencies globally when the project supports local isolation.
Using .env as a Python environment directory when secrets files may also use that name.
Running destructive cleanup commands without checking the target path.

faq.question: Does this skill install dependencies by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides setup guidance and commands. A user or agent must choose whether to run them.

faq.question: Which ecosystems does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers common Python, Node.js, Ruby, and Go project indicators.

faq.question: Can it choose between Poetry, Pipenv, and venv?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses files such as pyproject.toml, Pipfile, and requirements.txt to suggest a workflow.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium risk warning for package installation and cleanup commands.

faq.question: Does it read secret environment files?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The .env references are naming and gitignore examples.

faq.question: Should users approve commands first?
faq.answer: Yes. Users should review package installation and deletion commands before execution.
